1-1.v: Every business transaction can be expressed using one fundamental equation that keeps financial records in balance.

Assets are resources owned by the business (cash, inventory, equipment). Liabilities are what the business owes to outsiders (loans, accounts payable). Equity is the owners' residual interest — what's left after liabilities are subtracted from assets.

v: Assets: Cash, Accounts Receivable, Inventory, Equipment, Buildings, Prepaid Expenses
Liabilities: Accounts Payable, Notes Payable, Accrued Expenses, Unearned Revenue
Equity: Common Stock, Retained Earnings, Additional Paid-In Capital

1-1.v: The Equation Always Stays in Balance
Every transaction affects at least two accounts, but the equation always remains equal. This is the foundation of double-entry bookkeeping.
On the FMAA exam, when a question gives you any two elements of the accounting equation, you can always calculate the third. If Assets = $80,000 and Liabilities = $35,000, then Equity = $45,000.
Equity = Common Stock + Retained Earnings + Revenues − Expenses − Dividends
Revenues increase equity; expenses and dividends decrease it. This expanded view connects the income statement to the balance sheet.

1-2.v: Debits and Credits — The Language of Accounting
Every accounting entry has two sides: a debit (left side) and a credit (right side). The total debits must always equal total credits. This is double-entry bookkeeping.
Total Debits = Total Credits (in every journal entry)
Memory trick: Assets, Expenses, and Dividends have Debit normal balances — remember AED (like the UAE currency). Everything else (Liabilities, Equity, Revenue) has a Credit normal balance.
A company receives $5,000 cash from a customer for services rendered.

v: Identify what increased: Cash (Asset) increased → Debit Cash $5,000
Identify what else changed: Revenue increased → Credit Service Revenue $5,000

1-3.v: The accounting cycle begins with recording transactions in a journal, then posting those entries to the general ledger accounts.
The journal is the book of original entry. Every transaction is first recorded here in chronological order showing the date, accounts debited and credited, and amounts.

Date | Account (Debit) | Dr. Amount
      | Account (Credit) | | Cr. Amount
      | Explanation

After journalizing, entries are posted to the general ledger — a collection of individual T-accounts for every account used by the business. The T-account has debits on the left and credits on the right.

v: Record the transaction in the general journal
Post the debit amount to the left side of the debit account's T-account
Post the credit amount to the right side of the credit account's T-account
Calculate the running balance for each account

1-3.v: The FMAA exam may ask you to prepare a partial journal entry or identify what's missing. Always check that debits equal credits and that the accounts make logical sense for the transaction described.
